segments.text: I'm not going to talk about this, seriously.
Let me see Patrick.
Oh, yeah.
Oh.
Mm-mm.
You are really twisting something.
Yeah.
Well, me, you're too.
I'm just kidding.
Good morning.
Good morning.
All right.
We are here this morning in live public session
for the Estimates for Vote Labour Market,
in which we are hearing from the Minister of Education.
Thank you, Minister, for being here
and for all your officials as well.
Immigration, did I not say that?
Oh, did I? Oh, there you go. Sorry.
I already said a couple of words
around the wrong way earlier, so nothing special.
Minister of Immigration,
thank you very much for being here.
We have an hour with you this morning.
We're open to introductory remarks,
which we understand is a little extra to discuss,
and so we'll hand it over to you now,
and then we'll open for questions after that.
Well, thank you very much for the opportunity
to speak to you about Budget 2026
in the Immigration Portfolio.
I'm joined today by Nick Blakely,
Chief Executive of Embate.
As the committee have already been advised,
my opening remarks will be slightly longer than usual,
as there is a significant matter
regarding the scrapping of Immigrations
biometric capability upgrade project
that I want to bring to your attention.
This is a matter that I consider to be very serious,
and that requires substantive explanation,
both from myself and from Enby.
And as such, I'm going to focus my opening remarks on this,
and I'll leave it to the committee
to ask any other questions I would like about
other decisions in Budget 2026,
if you'd like to.
I'm also tabling with the committee right now
a review of the project that is being
publicly released now.
The BCU project
by Metropathically Upratox School at BCU from now on began in 2018 to upgrade and future-proof
immigration New Zealand's identity management systems, with a business case approved by
NB in 2019. Estimated whole-of-life costs were $19.49 million, seemingly at this point
with no ministerial oversight or sign-off of that $19 million. The project encountered
challenges almost immediately. Critical decisions were made at pace without proper analysis
due diligence. In the very early stages, there were already delays, unclear requirements,
rushed decisions, no proper governance and persistent disagreements. In 2020, the very
critical decision was made by NB to significantly change the scope of the project to also
include a workflow flow solution. So essentially taking it off the shelf solution and incorporating
it together with a workflow solution, drastically changing the scope of the project. It
significantly increased the complexity and the cost of the project, and it was done without
proper analysis, interrogation, or importantly ministerial engagement oversight or approval.
These flawed foundations led to ongoing delivery issues, extensive customization,
and a near constant stream of change requests. In total, more than 170 changes were raised,
signalling deep structural issues with the project. Major change requests significantly
altered scope, budget, timelines, and yet lacked analysis required to support these decisions.
Ministerial oversight was extremely limited, with most significant decisions being made
internally within NB. In November 2021, Minister Farfoy and the Minister of Finance,
Grant Robertson, noted the whole of life costs had increased to over $30 million, just noted.
Then in July 2023, Minister Little approved the increase to whole of life costs from the
project from $30 million to $35 million, from the limited information available to
me, it appears envy deliberately withheld information from those ministers and did not seek ministerial
approval for whole-of-life cost increases when it was required throughout the project.
It is also not clear what questions previous ministers asked about the project on going
delays and escalating costs or what information they received. Then just a month later,
in August 2023, Minister Little declined to go to Cabinet seeking another whole-of-life
increase to $40 million. In March 2024, officials recommended that I go to Cabinet seeking to
increase the whole-of-life costs again without telling me that Minister Little had previously
declined. Alongside this, importantly, they advised me that the project has undertaken
three independent quality assurance reviews, IQAs I'll call them. This last one being in
September 2023. The latest IQA confirmed the project approach was sound and robust.
The build was achievable and the risk management practice is effective. The project has implemented
the IQA recommendations and continually looks for opportunities to improve.
I wasn't satisfied with the advice that I'd received and so I asked a number of questions,
including seeking more information and asking for the IQA itself. It was at this point
it became apparent to me that there were not only serious issues with the project,
but what I had been advised about the IQA was complete fiction. Far from being sound and
robust, achievable and effective, what the September 2023 IQA actually said was,
given the project's poor delivery history, its ability, inability to meet agreed milestones,
we have doubts as to whether the project will in fact deliver at all.
And we question its continuation. I was, as I'm sure that you can appreciate,
furious, at being provided with advice that was diametrically opposed to the
truth on such a critical project. However, I was also advised that ending the project
would mean writing off $31 million of investment as well as exposing us to risks
around the current system failing, and that the project was able to be delivered in just a few
months. A range of...
significant interventions at this point were made, including an investigation by the chief
executive of MB. Another IQA was commissioned, which returned an updated status possible.
And I directed that an independent technology transformation expert be brought on the project
to advise me directly. The chief executive of MB decided with those interventions in
place alongside strength and governance to continue with the project given how advanced
it was and that they expected to be able to go live within a couple of months,
a few months, sorry. I was also advised that MB would be able to keep within its
originally within its whole of life set cost of 35 million. The project then made progress
through the remainder of 2024 and 2025. We received regular updates on the progress
from both MB and our independent advisor. And then in late 2025, as the project entered
user acceptance testing, performance testing and system integration testing,
problems compounded with more defects being created than were being resolved.
And ultimately, the decision was made by the MB acting chief executive in November 25.
The project was irrefutable, could not go live and needed to be ended. So after seven years
and $30 million, Budget 26 contains a repayable funding package that provides up to $31.2 million
to write off the project, a similar amount to what I was advised in March 2024.
Those costs well over the next few years have to be met by future users of the immigration system.
And it is totally unacceptable to me as a minister that after seven years,
MB somehow spent tens of millions of dollars. Not only do we have nothing to show for it,
but we are now in a position of having to maintain the existing aging infrastructure
while a new solution is sought. I have more to say, including about the subsequent review
and its damning findings that you now have. But I'm aware that the committee has already
given me an extended time, which I'm grateful for. And we can cover that in questions.
First question from Phil Twyford.
Minister, what is your view of MB's conduct through
throughout this episode, and what do you intend to do about it?
My view from what I have, the information that I have available, is that the report is very
clear that ministers were not given full information, that in cases we were misled, that people
were removed from the project when they asked questions about its viability.
And that the whole of life costs were not, they did not put to ministers to get proper
approval, and information wasn't given to ministers.
There's one more, the trifecta for me is people were moved sideways, ministers were misled
or given poor information, but there was a third thing that will come to me in a moment.
I have spoken to the Public Service Commissioner on Friday.
I've written to him, and I've asked him to undertake a review that he has agreed to,
an integrity review, the terms of reference we provided to my office in the coming days.
And I understand that MB will also be undertaking their own internal reviews as well,
which Nick can speak to.
Do you have a view about how high this conduct went in the management hierarchy of MB?
I don't, I'm not going to get into that, because I think that in the fullness of time
the report will show us that.
I remember the third thing, it's the trifecta.
Firstly, ministers were misled, secondly, people were moved from the project
when they raised concerns, and thirdly, and this is quite serious,
creative accounting practices have been undertaken to keep...
costs of the project below cabinet's mandated limit. Those are extraordinarily serious findings
of the report. They raise more questions than they answer, which is why I've asked Sir Brian to undertake
a review. Can I make a few comments? Thank you. I'm extremely disappointed that it's taken seven
years and $33 million and we have not delivered it. That's unacceptable. I found the review a
very difficult read. It finds serious failings and serious concerns about integrity, which the
minister has raised. It does find that ultimately it was the correct decision to terminate the project,
but that it could have happened much earlier. I take accountability for that is on behalf of
the department over a long period of time and I'm committed to learning from this and implementing
change. There are a number of actions underway. The first is Sir Brian's review into the integrity
issues raised by this. He will look into that. On the back of that, I will undertake employment
investigations if that's appropriate on the basis of what that integrity review finds.
I'm also taking a stocktake of all of our major technology projects to be assured that the
governance and process failings that were evident in this review are not to be repeated in any of
our other projects. I will also be strengthening our overall processes and getting independent
assurance that those processes are now adequate. One of the other major immigration projects
is the Future Services project and Sir Brian and the PSC will also be doing an assurance review
of that project. We're taking action to learn the lessons for this and to rectify things for the
future. I've already apologized to the ministers and I would apologize to New Zealanders who expect
this project. So minister you've you've alluded to the fact that because of the
immigration system and particularly tax payers as well will have to pick up the
tab 33 million dollars of waste of expenditure. Are there any other
implications for users of the immigration system in terms of operational
benefits the fact that the planned benefits of the upgrade won't be
whether the expected time frame. Yes and I can get officials to talk in more detail
but at a very high level we've made sure that the existing technology is able to
be maintained. When we officials are at the moment working on what is what a new
solution is that they're going to be providing information to me in the near
future and at that point we will take further decisions on what more we need
to do to stabilise the existing technology but the advice that I've got is
at the moment the technology is stable and we likely will have to upgrade it
somewhat depending on depending on the solution that we choose for the upgrade
we may have to do a little bit more maintenance on it to make sure that
it is stable because it is a very core part of our system. And do you have a
plan to replace the upgrade project with a new project to deliver what
was hoped for? Yeah I can talk to that. So yes the same issues that were the
rationale for undertaking this project still exists in that our existing
biometric capability is old it is on systems that need upgrading so that
still exists that's in place today it is still working it will need some
additional investment over the coming years for it to keep going but
there is still a case for having a more modern platform and that is the
work that we are now undertaking that will be reporting to ministers in the
next few months on.
You've given us a damning indictment of the conduct of even senior public servants in the MB.
What questions are you asking about the behaviour and conduct of your officials outside of this project?
If this behaviour has been demonstrated in relation to one IT project,
what confidence do you have that these behaviours are not taking place in others?
Well, you may well remember Minister Andrew Little going down to the tiles
and asking for an investigation in the dying days of the previous Labour Government
on immigration because he wasn't getting the information that he needed
and that led to the best work review, which again was very damning.
And at that point, when I became the Minister, I said we've got a relationship to repair
and my confidence at this point is low and we're going to need to repair that over the next few years.
And at that point was very clear, bring out everything that I need to see
and we've done some great work over the last couple of years.
But I am at a point now where that feels like it's been completely undone and undermined
because of this that was not raised with me, even though we were unpicking it
and finding out bits and pieces along the way it was never fully disclosed.
And also the fact that this report, you'll note, was dated two months ago
and I received it on Friday.
So those behaviours are seemingly still there and that's why I want a full investigation
because while we've done a huge amount of great work in the policy space
over the last couple of years, my confidence has been knocked again,
which is very unfortunate.
So you've asked the Public Service Commission to do an investigation
but what are you going to do about your department?
Well, I can ask for the investigation and wait for the outcome of that
but in the meantime, Nick has...
has assured me that he's doing his part as well.
And I've already insisted that we
are doing an overview of the current IT project
upgrade, which is the R-Future Systems, which you'll know,
and love is adept, moving things on to that
to make sure that that business case is where it needs to be,
that we're getting the outcomes that we expected
and that that project is delivering as expected.
So my key concerns lie in the IT space,
which is why I've asked for a full update of what's
going on in those spaces as well.
But we're talking about behaviors in relation to an IT project,
but are you confident those same behaviors aren't
being exhibited in the policy advice you're
getting about compliance and enforcement,
about the issuing of visas, about the humanitarian side
of the department's work?
I mean, the advice that I've been getting
from policy, I've been confident in.
We've done a huge amount of good work over the last few years,
and you see that from the turnaround of migrant
exploitation, of numbers, of skills.
And I'm very happy with that work.
However, there is certainly a side in NB.
And remembering that this IT department
sits outside of immigration itself
in the wider NB environment.
And this is something that none of my DCEs
had a direct line of sight over.
And that's part of the problem.
But the advice I've been getting on policy
relating to our work program over the last two years,
I've been very satisfied with.
OK, thank you.
Because I take integrity very seriously,
and that's why I welcome the public service commissioners
review.
I see expectations with my team.
This is not where we should be as a department.
And so I will be doing everything
I can to give a fair statement.
A supplementary question.
Thanks, here.
I've got a couple of clarifying questions coming out of what?
I'm pretty upright.
just situations being read.
Am I correct in understanding that the $31.2 million,
I think I heard that's getting written off,
is approximately where the cost was at when you became
the minister?
The advice I received on the 28th of March
was that that would be the write-off cost.
But to be honest with you, I think
there is lots of questions to be asked
around the actual costs of this project
because of the report and its claims
around creative accounting.
So I think there'll be a lot to be dug out.
Obviously, ministers, you have to go on the advice
that they're given.
The 28th of March sent a $31 million write-off cost,
which is significant, especially given
where it was so close to the end of the project.
But yes, those two figures are very similar.
There was a number of things that Enby
were doing in the background to recover.
They were getting clawing back funds from the vendor
and assuring me they could work within the whole of life
costs that had already been set.
On page 18, I've just quickly read this report.
It says that with some staff characterizing
financial management practices as creative accounting,
largely driven by efforts to avoid exceeding
the cabinet-level funding threshold.
That's a pretty outrageous statement to make in a report.
As the minister, how does that make you feel
about the department's performance?
Well, this is almost as bad as it gets,
avoiding cabinet scrutiny.
There is more to this, though, and uncovering
all of the documents that previous ministers signed out
when it came to whole of life costs, it was minimal.
There was only two signouts by ministers
across the life of the project when it went from $19 million
to $35 million eventually.
There was only two signouts.
And it's supposed to be.
to be signed out every time that goes, the whole of life cost goes up as far as I've been advised
and that seemingly did not happen and even when it did happen the advice was very minimal and I'll
give you an example of one of those where Minister Farf always signed out a piece of paper that that's
how big it is, it's a pamphlet, it doesn't provide any of the advice and I've not seen
any other advice that's been able to be provided to me that was received by either him or the
Minister of Finance so the advice was very scant it did not lay out the history of the
project or the increase in costs and actually by the time it seems from what I can see the only
I'm only going on the information I've got by the time they were asked to to sign out an approval
to go over 20 million dollars a whole of life cost it had already gone to 30 million so there's
there is a whole lot of things that needs to be looked into because I don't think that ministers
were getting the information and the advice that they should have got when making these decisions
I will certainly be asking more questions to the department later but a question for you
how do you think Merlin, the physiotherapist and woman who bring in physios from overseas or Patrick
who's bringing in IRC workers for forestry or Neil and Harah who's bringing in Filipinos as
managers are going to feel when they understand that they're going to be paying not only for the
past project on those visa costs but the next one and as much as Nick I respect the fact that you've
acknowledged that you're not happy with this has it actually been worked out as a what it's going
to cost per person what this is actually costing every one of those visa holders in the future
to pay for this mistake look no it hasn't we haven't got to that stage yet in fact we haven't
actually done the write-off as yet and I think there will be more to come out of this actual
costs and of course we don't know what the future
an IT solution will look like at this point or what the costs will be of
maintaining the current biometric capability system, but it certainly is a
very difficult pill to swallow that those costs will be borne by future users
of the system, as I said in my opening speech.
Supplementary, Ricardo.
Yeah, just good a few questions. Thank you.
On page 17, the report notes that the cybersecurity risks were significant.
Were you at any point being made aware that there were high cybersecurity
risks attached to this project?
Well, the whole point of the project is to avoid those.
And one of the things that we were advised in early 2024 was the fact
that if we didn't complete the project, then those risks would be born.
And that's quite a serious thing, not only the right of the $31 million,
but also the fact that we had a legacy system that at the time
we were told was at serious risk and those and those would be the implications.
But I mean, what does it say that in this project with NEC, like there were
a lot of cybersecurity risks that were significant according to the report
and you were kept out of the loop when it's something that could massively
impact people's rights to privacy or information?
Does it does it not raise significant concerns that there were
cybersecurity risks attached to this project and you were not made aware of those?
There's lots of concerns.
I'll probably get into respect to this because it's quite technical if that's OK.
Yeah, just to clarify, this was of the potential solution.
So it was never put into practice.
So the concerns were about this, the work that was being done on the project
so that they were never exposed into individuals and our existing system is still in place.
And can I just ask what communications did NB or yourself minister have
with any migration five partners in relationship to, for example,
this project or the issues that have arisen since the report and what feedback have you received?
if, you know, I have other migration five countries had similar issues with this company or with
trying to update the systems, which would be able to align any sort of communications you've
had with the partners that would have been part of the secure real time platform information
sharing network.
So I'm not sure about M5 communication over the period of us, we can get back to that.
On the, I may just say something about the vendor and the nature of this project, there
were there were two main parts to the project.
There was a core functionality around image matching, essentially, so you are an intensity
matching.
And then there's how you then, the workflow was the minister mentioned at the start about
how you integrate that into these systems and the core functionality, you know, any
C would be regarded as global best practice.
They're actually that functionalities of the core of our existing system.
It's also, I believe, in DIAs, all of government solution, social recognition as a service.
So what was unique about this was building, asking the vendor to build that referral
that was, I understand, unique at the time.
And that was, as the minister said, the core mistake early on was to go down that
route.
So just to distinguish between the core technology that NEC holds versus this
particular workflow that was being developed.
So a very succinct supplementary, Vanessa, thank you, minister.
Yeah, have you any concerns in this project about fraud?
Is there any potential for that given that one of the project costs, as said, create
accounting.
So inconsistent financial reporting, make it a definitive view of actual
expenditure and movements of 4.44 million transferring between.
Are you confident that there has been no fraud in this?
Look, I can't speak to Ford, the PSC will do their investigation, but from the information
that I've been provided, what I can tell so far, and I still don't have a full picture,
and from what I've been told, things were done that required cabinet oversight in order
to keep it away from Cabinet.
So splitting the project into two parts again and keeping it like that, a required cabinet
oversight.
But for some reason, that decision was taken by the government's body overseeing
the project and kept away from ministers.
So all I can go by is the report that I received literally a couple of days ago
before you have now seen it, but this is part of the investigation that the PSC
will need to undertake.
OK, quickly grant.
Thank you.
Thank you.
It's a very confusing situation.
But then I'm just, what surprises me is that at no point within the ministry itself,
envy itself, that there was not a big red flags coming up to the senior management
level, particularly this project, it's seven years, it seems to be an ardent
long time to work through a project of this sort of nature.
Any comment on that?
I mean, I think this is where the review finds the serious failings in the
oversight and governance, that it didn't have the sufficient oversight.
In fact, decisions went around governance.
There wasn't sufficient reporting to ministers.
That wasn't even always correct when it did hurt to ministers.
So it's those systems and processes that were not in place, were not adhered to.
That is the review find that behind the failings.
Thank you.
I have a final supplement.
Thank you.
Since this finding, are there any other technology project systems and
processes that are being red flagged?
So we have a number of technology programs.
There is, I do recall, one of them not in the immigration space is subject to
examination at the moment about that one's on track. But that is why I want to leave an overall
stock take of all of the projects. Do they have the appropriate governance oversight,
reporting some ministers, if there are any concerns, we flag those up.
So for stock take, how far back is it going to look at?
That's the current project, all of the current in-train technology projects.
Okay, just before we move on, Ricardo.
Yeah, the reporters are pretty damning on any see the company.
And just from a quick search, it seems like it's used by several other countries.
Do you have any concerns based on the experience that in behind with this company that, you know,
this being used by other countries, which we have information sharing regimes with that
risks attached with us sharing information with countries that
have an alignment with a company that has had a multitude of issues with its ability to,
you know, deploy this kind of technology issues with cybersecurity.
And do you not have any concerns about the fact that other countries that we share information
with are using NEC as well? I'll start with high level and I'll get Nick to expand.
But if we had stuck with the original scope of buying the off the shelf project product,
it's very likely that none of this would have happened.
The core critical problem was in 2020 when MB took the decision to turn what was an off the shelf
perfectly usable IT system and merge it with a workflow system that had not been done before.
So the advice that I've received is that it is used by us, it's used by other countries
and we'll have to continue to use it. The critical failure was that merging that in hindsight
should never have happened. And that's what caused all of the potential problems, which is why we
we couldn't integrate it at the end and why it eventually
wasn't able to be integrated with the system.
Had it been integrated into the system,
then you would have seen some of those issues.
Yeah, it goes back to the best two parts.
Is the core technology, the ability
to do the facial matching, that's
what's used around the world.
It was this workflow path, and that's
where the cyber security issues come from,
because they come from interface with the rest
of the world.
Would you mind just expanding on exactly why
the relationship between immigration in New Zealand
and NEC was strained?
The bottom page forward just has a line on that,
but that seems quite serious, that not only was that a lot of this
was kept from the ministers, but that even throughout the
relationship between Ayn said and NEC was not good.
Do you mind just broadening what issues immigration in New
Zealand encountered in dealing with the company?
Yeah, I think this goes back to the
setting what the project was not set up well.
And so then what needed to happen
was a number of changes through the process.
It wasn't clear on the requirements at the start.
Again, there were a few thoughts about that.
And all of those things meant for a strange relationship,
because you're constantly changing what's required
and renegotiating.
It's those sorts of things all along the way,
trying to resolve those defects, perhaps differences
of view about how best to resolve those.
I'm not close to all the ins and outs,
but it's those types of things made for a very challenging
relationship.
As you saw, as I mentioned when I started this,
over 117 change requests does not make for a great relationship
or a stable project.
But I think that the key thing that the report finds
was around right at the very beginning,
the business case, lacked the detailed analysis
and required to make support decision making.
It lacked due diligence, market testing,
and despite the scale of complexity of the project.
So you can see that right from the business case, not being set up well, and then that
massive change of scope in 2020, essentially, of the Shelf Act and merged it with the workflow
system was a critical failure.
Before we move on, can I just ask why was the Minister not aware of the report for two
and a half months?
That's the one thing that's sticking out that hasn't been answered for me.
So that's on us. We received the report, and my focus actually at the time was fully
on the fuel response. The team at the time decided that it would be better to have a
response to the report at the same time reporting to Ministers. That was the wrong call, but
that's why that was made, and it took too long to get through.
Can I just note, though, in that preceding two months, there was no response provided.
Yeah, we've noted that.
Right. Next primary, new topic.
Thank you.
Two years ago, Minister, you substantially increased immigration fees.
Dumbled, doubled many of them, which was a big shock to many users of the immigration
system, but it was done on the basis that the system should be financially sustainable
and largely funded by its users. Less than two years later, the budget provides $31.2
million of ground support and access to an $84 million contingency.
What are the principal reasons that the system is currently not financially
self-sustaining? Were visa revenues low as an expected expenditure higher than expected?
The advice that I've received, and I'll get officials to come and speak to this as well,
the main contributing factor is the volume of the AEW visa was far, far lower than they predicted
over that time. They lower expected volumes. They also failed to take into
account the products that we don't charge for because of bilateral relationships or you'll know
you'll be aware of them as to why there's many of them and potentially a bit of an over-optimism
of what the visa volumes would be but I have been assured and I've been driving this myself to
ensure that we are improving our productivity and I've been advised that their internal budgets
have had good cost controls. It's not usual for a minister to get involved in operations
but when I discovered that, for example, the Christchurch branch had very low productivity
in terms of their job check, for example, they're processing on average of one a day or something
similar whereas Auckland were doing four a day, immediately ensured that that was dealt with
and so the systems and processes in immigration now are much different making sure we're improving
productivity. So while we've had declining numbers of visas over this difficult fiscal period
the team have been making sure that we are improving productivity and output so that
we are competitive. The issues lie with reduced numbers of visas and also which we probably
should have expected before we made the change. We had a massive surge, you may remember, in
visitor visa applications so a whole lot of visa applications massive surge took us a while to get
through but then obviously didn't see the benefits of those increases so the team have gone away
and ensured that they're on top of those now for the next review. Would you agree that it amounts
to a pretty significant failure of forecasting and if so what are you doing about improving
the department's financial forecasting? Well yes it is a failure and I think they will admit that
themselves and we've made sure that in the upcoming fee and levy review that all of the
things that were not calculated correctly will be
dealt with. I would say though that it is like Treasury encounter occasionally it is very difficult
to predict the future in terms of visa volumes. They do their very best. I don't think we definitely
didn't get it right. Could we have done better? Absolutely, but there is an element of crystal
ballgazing when it comes to visa volumes but I know that the team have gone away and worked
on what went wrong last time to make sure that that's moved out. So who do you expect to
ultimately bear the cost of restoring the immigration visa memorandum account? Employers,
migrants or taxpayers? Well it'll be the future users of the immigration system because we are a
self funding immigration system and those costs will be borne by future users of the immigration
system. So in saying that are you signalling hefty future increases in visa fees and levies to be
paid by migrants and employers over the next year or two? Not necessarily and this is outside of my
expertise and I can get the team come up and talk about it but we are of course forecasting
higher visa volumes and you may remember in 2021 when a similar write-off or similar loan
was done in terms of a write-off. It only accounted for part of the deficit of the two accounts.
The remaining balance of those accounts was borne by future visa holders and an increased
number of people coming through the system and that's what you will see here as visa numbers

productivity of our different offices and the team are doing a really good job of that.

Good morning, Chair and Minister of the Committee.
As Associate Minister of Immigration, I'm responsible for our refugee program and for the work combating,
trafficking and persons.
I'm just going to make some brief comments.
The nature of forced displacement and migration continues to evolve, with record numbers of
people worldwide seeking safety and better opportunities.
As global responses change, New Zealand is now the third largest resettlement country
after Canada and Australia have been menstruating sustained leadership in an increasingly uncertain
international environment.
I'd like to stress that that isn't the third largest per capita, with the global system
under strain.
New Zealand continues to meet its resettlement commitments, and we take the third largest
number of UNHCR refugees.
Our approach remains rooted in our values of fairness, dignity and support for those
in need.
If we review our strategies and programs from the refugee quota program, family support and
our work with asylum seekers and victims of trafficking, we reaffirm the dedication to
providing pathways for protection and integration.
New Zealand maintains a strong and long-standing relationship with the UNHCR, working together
to support and protect the rights and wellbeing of refugees and displaced populations
globally.
I'm pleased that INZ has met the annual target of proving 600 people under the Refugee
Family Support Programme and the Refugee Quota Programme, reaching the on-track
to meeting the 1500.
I'm also pleased to inform you of
progress made of those individuals who have been waiting in the Tier 2 registration queue.
As at May 2025, there was 3,737 people in the Tier 2 registration queue. As at the same
date this year, the number has dropped to 2,180. Finally, as you know in December 2025,
the government released the new All of Government Transnational Serious and Organised Crime
Strategy and Action Plan. This aims to create a more cohesive response to transnational
Serious Organised Crime. One of the actions in the strategy pertains to trafficking in
persons with INZ leading the development of a standalone Trafficking in Persons National
Action Plan. This plan will provide a prioritised and coordinated multi-agency
approach to enhancing New Zealand's response to trafficking. Thank you.
Thank you very much. We have a first question from Phil Twyford.

that reduction is due to people who have been processed, their registrations

that the sponsor is no longer eligible to be a sponsor. There's been a range
of things that have impacted on that total number. I would refer to the fact
that we've had 80...
registrations which is 316 people have resulted in an invitation to apply to apply 36 registrations
which is 185 people were declined and I might return to officials around their numbers of

and when we assess that sponsor for eligibility it's around half of registrations and a quarter

sometimes they don't meet the criteria to have lived here in the last three years and sometimes

has been worked through. So this is why Immigration New Zealand, we have committed that to the policy

Radio New Zealand reported recently that around 20 asylum seekers have been detained in corrections

Since January 2024, how many asylum seekers have been physically accommodated in corrections
facilities?
I think I'll have to refer to officials for the number.
I can tell you that since the decision making, the freedom of movement decision making panel
was established in 2022 that 21 individuals have claimed asylum while already subject
to warrants of commitment with one individual claiming asylum during the process of obtaining
a warrant.
So where a claimant is in detention under a warrant of commitment, when they claim asylum
the panel's in principle decision in all cases is to have them released on conditions.
However in some cases this kind of occur immediately because we have to find them suitable
housing for which they can be released.
So we try and do that as soon as possible.
I know that doesn't quite answer your question in terms of the timing, but.
So since 2022, 21 individuals applied for asylum while they were already subject to a warrant
of commitment.
That's right.
What kind of warrant of commitment and for example would they have been subject
to?
I don't have that data, I'm afraid.
But an immigration related warrant.
Okay, so the decision-making panel decided that asylum seekers should not be detained.
At what length of time were these 21 individuals held?
How long were they held in prison before?
I think the specificity of that data would have to come back to you and WPQ.
We don't have it at hand.
I don't have all the data for all 21.
Did I hear correctly that the incentive of the policy would be to have people released as soon as possible?
Yes, and I think there were some changes to the Immigration Act this February
to improve protections for people that have claimed refugee or protected person status
and are subject to a warrant of commitment.
So I think this was recommended in the KCW.
So it has bespoke settings for the detention of asylum claimants
and tries to take account of their particular vulnerabilities.
So during this period, my right is thinking that there have been no detention of asylum seekers
in corrections facilities unless they were already subject to a pre-existing warrant of commitment.
That's my understanding.
OK. What are the circumstances where one of these 21 asylum seekers,
what justification would there have been to keep them in prison?
It's just a case of finding them accommodation that they can move to
so we can't let them out to make them homeless.
So that's the consideration.
We try and do it as fast as possible,
but it depends a little bit about where in the country they are.
If they're in Auckland, it's quite a lot easier.
We have a...

or are we talking weeks or months that they would have been detained in corrections?
I'd say a few days to two weeks, but not longer, typically.
It's certainly something we've had to do.
And we can come back with the data for the next time.

that you would bring the committee a breakdown of,

So, Minister, in your opening remarks,
you talked about what's been a pretty extraordinary deterioration
internationally in the way that countries
approach forced displacement.
The kind of bizarre outcome that New Zealand is now
the third largest re-severment country, which
never believed that five years ago or ten years ago.
And I just, my question is really whether you or members
of the department have what about what needs to change,
given that the international environment's been deteriorating
so sharply, the 1951 Convention is sort of more or less
falling apart before our eyes.
And yet there's not quite a record.
There's been a slight reduction in the number of people displaced
globally, but it's still in the region of, you know,
between 110 and 120 million people.
How should a country like New Zealand be responding to this
kind of crisis?
It was actually, I had the privilege of visiting Geneva
and visiting UNHCR and Red Cross earlier this year,
I think it was.
And I think that there is, you know, in my discussions with
UNHCR and you probably all have found it, there is huge work
that can...
be done to strengthen countries to allow people to remain or return. I think that that's a pathway
of work that is important in this space. From my discussions with UNHCA and Red Cross, the
economic component of those refugees, so it's moving because they can't grow food, etc.,
those sort of components is a significant issue. So I think the more we can do around our
leaning into stabilising these environments so that people aren't forced to leave. I think there is
work in that space that will continue to be very important. I think the companion pathway work,
which has now talked about cause, a number of countries are working on those companion pathways,
those other options, so skill migration and those sort of beyond the refugee placement
program, those other opportunities, workforce mobility, that sort of activity. So there is a
range of things that I know and it is a reality. I don't think there's one fix, but I think that
focusing on strengthening those vulnerable economies and those vulnerable places will
help to some extent. The other discussion in this has not been sort of policy work or anything,
but one of the discussions that I thought was really useful was actually talking about
those that are working in this space, particularly in the UNHCA and Red Cross environment, is that
rather than everybody crossing over each other and everybody doing a little bit in the same place,
is actually dividing up our responses. So different agencies in different countries focus
on specific areas rather than us all doing a little bit in one place. There was some sort of
dialogue around that, which I think makes some sense around actually, you know, who's going
to do what and what area, rather than us all going to one place and doing a lot and then
moving to another place, you know, actually sharing that. And that came from the Red Cross
initiatives in that space. So there is, you know, I think dialogue to have in that
area.
Have you asked for any advice on complementary pathways, so labour market mobility or education
as a way of...
At this point, I'm really focused on getting the cause decisions through. And
that is, you know, the first cab off the rank. I think that that is the priority for
us to give stability in that space. Then moving forward. And this is where,
although it's not relevant, but this is where I feel very strongly around the
work we're doing in Trafficking because, you know, this displaced community
becomes the most vulnerable and the most exposed to that kind of criminal
activity. So that becomes, you know, the next piece of work that I'm really
focused on.

It's just the new primary. Yeah, go for it.
